content.problemStatement:
You have a grid with NNN rows and MMM columns. You have two types of tiles — one of dimensions 2×22 \times 22×2 and the other of dimensions 1×11 \times 11×1. You want to cover the grid using these two types of tiles in such a way that:


Each cell of the grid is covered by exactly one tile; and
The number of 1×11 \times 11×1 tiles used is minimized.
Find the **minimum** number of 1×11 \times 11×1 tiles you have to use to fill the grid.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line containing two space-separated integers N,MN, MN,M.


Output Format
For each test case, print on a new line the minimum number of 1×11 \times 11×1 tiles needed to fill the grid.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and M denoting the number of rows and columns of the grid.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum possible number of 1×1 tiles required to completely fill the N×M grid.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For each grid we apply the formula ans = (N%2)*M + (M%2)*N - (N%2)*(M%2):
- 1×1 → both odd → 1+1-1 = 1
- 4×5 → N even, M odd → ans = N = 4
- 6×8 → both even → ans = 0
- 3×2 → N odd, M even → ans = M = 2
7×9: both odd → 7+9-1 = 15.\n8×13: N even, M odd → ans = N = 8.

When one dimension is 1, only 1×1 tiles can be used.
- 1×10000 (odd×even) → ans = M = 10000.
- 10000×1 (even×odd) → ans = N = 10000.
- 10000×10000 both even → ans = 0.

solution.approach:
A 2×2 tile covers four cells and can only be placed when both dimensions are at least 2. To minimise the number of 1×1 tiles we should place as many 2×2 tiles as possible. The maximal number of 2×2 tiles is floor(N/2) * floor(M/2). All cells that are not covered by these tiles lie in the last row (if N is odd) and/or the last column (if M is odd). The count of uncovered cells is:
  uncovered = (N % 2) * M + (M % 2) * N - (N % 2) * (M % 2)
The subtraction removes the double‑counted corner cell when both N and M are odd. This formula directly gives the minimum number of 1×1 tiles.
The algorithm for each test case is O(1).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); M = int(next(it))
        ans = (N%2)*M + (M%2)*N - (N%2)*(M%2)
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
